title: Allium × proliferum
what_description: Allium × proliferum is a hybrid species of the genus Allium, belonging to the family Amaryllidaceae. It is a perennial bulbous plant characterized by linear, flat, and narrow leaves, typically growing 30-60 cm in height. The plant produces a scape with a dense umbel of white to pale purple flowers, each 10-15 mm in diameter, in late spring. The flowers are hexagonal in shape, with six petals and six stamens, and are arranged in a spherical, densely packed cluster. The plant thrives in full sun to partial shade, in well-drained soil, and is native to the Iberian Peninsula.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Allium × proliferum are inflammation, which is alleviated by its anti-inflammatory properties, cancer prevention, due to its ability to inhibit cell proliferation and induce apoptosis in cancer cells, diabetes, as it lowers blood sugar levels and improves insulin sensitivity, and cardiovascular disease, by reducing atherosclerosis, triglycerides, and LDL cholesterol. Allium × proliferum also helps with high blood pressure, as it relaxes blood vessels and reduces sodium levels. Furthermore, it treats digestive issues, such as gastritis, by protecting the gastric mucosa and reducing acid production, and ulcers, due to its antibacterial properties. Additionally, it has antioxidant properties, which counteract oxidative stress, and wound healing, due to its ability to accelerate tissue repair. Allium × proliferum also exhibits antifungal properties, preventing fungal infections, and is used to treat respiratory issues, including colds, cough, and influenza, by reducing inflammation and mucus production, and it helps alleviate sore throat and fever.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Allium × proliferum are its antimicrobial properties, which have been attributed to the presence of compounds such as allicin and diallyl disulfide, that inhibit the growth of bacteria and fungi. This plant also exhibits antioxidant properties, which can be attributed to the flavonoids and phenolic acids present in it, that help in scavenging free radicals and reducing oxidative stress. Additionally, Allium × proliferum has anti-inflammatory properties due to the presence of saponins and volatile oils, which can reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with various diseases. Furthermore, the antiviral properties of this plant have been demonstrated in its ability to inhibit the replication of certain viruses, attributed to the presence of sulfur-containing compounds and other bioactive molecules. The antifungal properties of Allium × proliferum, also attributed to allicin and other sulfur-containing compounds, make it a potential natural remedy for fungal infections.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Allium × proliferum are allicin, quercetin, diallyl trisulfide, kaempferol, and diallyl disulfide. Allicin has antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antiviral properties, and is responsible for the characteristic odor and flavor of garlic. Quercetin, a powerful flavonoid antioxidant, has anti-inflammatory and antiviral effects, and may help to protect against cardiovascular disease and cancer. Diallyl trisulfide and diallyl disulfide, both sulfur-containing compounds, have been shown to have anti-cancer properties, inducing apoptosis in cancer cells and inhibiting the growth of new blood vessels that feed tumors. Kaempferol, a flavonoid with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity, has been demonstrated to have neuroprotective effects and may help to prevent age-related cognitive decline.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Allium × proliferum are commonly utilized due to its pungent flavor and potential health benefits. Garlic oil is extracted from the plant through a process of cold-pressing or solvent extraction, and is used in cooking and as a medicinal agent. Pickled garlic is prepared by soaking the plant in a brine solution, which enhances its flavor and extends its shelf life. Garlic powder and garlic salt are obtained through the dehydration and salting of the plant, respectively, and are used as seasonings in various dishes. Garlic confit is a preparation of slow-cooked garlic in oil, which is used to add flavor and depth to a variety of cuisines.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Allium × proliferum improperly include experiences allergic reactions such as hives, itching, and swelling, as well as experiencing diarrhea due to its high sulfur content which can disrupt gut health. Exhibiting skin irritation, such as redness and blistering, is also a possible outcome, particularly in individuals who are sensitive to the compounds found in the plant. Furthermore, individuals who develop allergic reactions to Allium × proliferum may experience symptoms such as difficulty breathing, rapid heartbeat, and a drop in blood pressure, which can be life-threatening if left untreated. This is because the compounds in the plant can trigger an overactive immune response, leading to inflammation and other complications. Additionally, skin irritation caused by Allium × proliferum can increase the risk of infections and scarring if proper wound care is not taken.
uses: Allium × proliferum, also known as the walking onion, has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits. The plant's bulb and leaves are said to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. Traditionally, it has been used to treat various health issues, including skin conditions, colds, and respiratory problems. Some studies have also suggested that the plant may have antioxidant and anti-cancer properties. Further research is needed to confirm the plant's medicinal uses and potential health benefits.
taxonomy: Allium × proliferum is a hybrid of the Allium species, specifically a cross between Allium neapolitanum and Allium cepa. It is commonly known as the walking onion or Egyptian walking onion. This plant is classified as a member of the Amaryllidaceae family and is native to parts of Europe and Asia. Its classification is further broken down into the genus Allium and the species A. × proliferum. Within the kingdom Plantae, A. × proliferum is a perennial species that belongs to the monocot order Asparagales.
benefits: Allium × proliferum is known for its potential health benefits, which are mainly attributed to its rich content of antioxidants and vitamins. The plant contains compounds that may help reduce inflammation and improve cardiovascular health. Its antibacterial properties can aid in fighting off infections and promoting wound healing. Additionally, Allium × proliferum has been traditionally used to support digestive health and alleviate symptoms of bloating and gas. More research is needed to fully understand the extent of its health benefits.
parts: Allium × proliferum, the medicinal parts of the plant, are primarily used in traditional medicine. The bulbs, leaves, and flowers of Allium × proliferum are often used to treat various health conditions. In herbal medicine, the plant is valued for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant's medicinal parts are rich in sulfur compounds, which are believed to contribute to its therapeutic effects. These compounds are also responsible for the plant's distinctive onion-like aroma and flavor.
